question: Why is running a container with the --privileged flag dangerous?

options.text: The container gets all capabilities and access to host devices, enabling container escape
The container starts consuming more resources
options.explanation: --privileged doesn't affect resource consumption. The danger is that the container gets full access to the host system

options.text: The container becomes visible on the internet
options.explanation: --privileged doesn't affect network visibility. It gives the container all Linux capabilities and access to host devices

options.text: Docker automatically disables --privileged for security
options.explanation: Docker does NOT disable --privileged automatically. The user is responsible for security

question: What security measures are recommended for production containers?

options.text: Run processes as a non-privileged user (USER in Dockerfile)
Use read-only filesystem (--read-only)
Drop unnecessary Linux capabilities (--cap-drop ALL)
Mount Docker socket (/var/run/docker.sock) for monitoring
options.explanation: Mounting Docker socket gives full control over the Docker daemon — this is one of the main attack vectors, not a security measure

question: What is a supply chain attack in the Docker context?

options.text: An attack through a compromised base image or dependency containing malicious code
A DDoS attack on Docker Hub
options.explanation: DDoS on Docker Hub is an infrastructure attack, not a supply chain attack. Supply chain means injecting malicious code through dependencies

options.text: An attack on the network between containers
options.explanation: That's a network attack. Supply chain attack is compromising images or dependencies in the supply chain

options.text: Leaking environment variables from a container
options.explanation: ENV leakage is a different type of vulnerability. Supply chain attack means using infected images or packages

question: What tools are used for scanning vulnerabilities in Docker images?

options.text: Trivy (Aqua Security)
Docker Scout (built into Docker Desktop)
Snyk Container
Hadolint
options.explanation: Hadolint is a Dockerfile linter checking best practices. For vulnerability scanning use Trivy, Docker Scout, Snyk

question: Why are distroless images more secure than Alpine?

options.text: Distroless contains no shell, package manager, or utilities — an attacker has no tools inside the container
Distroless encrypts all files inside the image
options.explanation: Distroless doesn't encrypt files. Security comes from minimalism: no shell, no apt/apk, no attack utilities

options.text: Distroless automatically updates all dependencies
options.explanation: Distroless doesn't auto-update dependencies. Its advantage is the minimal attack surface

options.text: Distroless runs in a separate namespace from other containers
options.explanation: All containers run in separate namespaces. Distroless advantage is the absence of tools an attacker could use
